did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep transfer learning-based framework utilizing the YOLOv12-seg architecture for automated multi-class skin lesion recognition and instance segmentation. The model was trained on a curated dataset of 2,039 dermoscopic and clinical images to classify lesions into four categories (Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions, Cowpox, and Dermatofibroma) and achieved an mAP@50 of 0.816.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study proposing a deep transfer learning-based framework (YOLOv12-seg) for multi-class skin lesion recognition. It details the curation of a dataset (2,039 images), model training methodology (87%-8%-4% split), and quantitative evaluation metrics (mAP@50, precision, recall) on a held-out test set. Although published in the proceedings of the ICAUC-2026 conference, it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Transfer Learning–based Multi-Class Skin Lesion Recognition and Segmentation for Clinical Decision Support
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on AI-Driven Smart Systems and Ubiquitous Computing (ICAUC-2026)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep transfer learning-based framework using the YOLOv12-seg architecture for automated multi-class skin lesion recognition and instance segmentation. The model was trained on a curated dataset of 2,039 dermoscopic and clinical images covering four categories: Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions, Cowpox, and Dermatofibroma. Evaluated on a held-out test set with an 87%-8%-4% split, the framework achieved an overall mAP@50 of 0.816 and an mAP@50–95 of 0.514, with precision and recall values of 0.778 and 0.750, respectively. Class-wise analysis indicated strong performance for Vascular Lesions and Melanocytic Nevi, while qualitative results confirmed accurate lesion localization and segmentation boundaries. The system demonstrated high computational efficiency with millisecond-level inference and was deployed via a Streamlit-based web application for real-time clinical decision support.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class diagnostic labels (Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions, Cowpox, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for skin lesions. The study focuses on multi-class skin lesion recognition, evaluating performance using precision, recall, and confusion matrices for class-wise prediction accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Instance segmentation masks and bounding boxes
all_task_types.details: The model performs instance segmentation to delineate lesion boundaries and localize lesions within the image. Performance is evaluated using mAP@50 and mAP@50-95 for both bounding box detection and mask segmentation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC / MILK10k Dataset
datasets_used.description: A primary dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing 10,480 images with paired clinical and dermoscopic views, rich metadata, and expert-annotated lesion masks under the MONET framework. It covers 11 diagnostic categories including melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions.

use.training: Source for curated final dataset; samples were filtered and integrated to build the final training/validation/testing sets.
use.other: Source for pixel-level segmentation masks and diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: Skin Lesion Dataset (SKIN-DS)
datasets_used.description: A dataset curated by Ahmed and hosted on Kaggle, consisting of approximately 3,000 dermoscopic images spanning 14 diagnostic categories. It was constructed by merging the HAM10000 (2019) and MSLDv2.0 datasets.

use.training: Source for curated final dataset; samples were filtered and integrated.
use.other: Source for diverse lesion categories and viral conditions.

datasets_used.name: Skin Tumor Classification TensorFlow Notebook Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising over 36,000 images across ten dermatological categories. In this study, only melanocytic nevi samples were retained from this source to strengthen benign lesion representation.

use.training: Source for curated final dataset; only melanocytic nevi samples were used.

datasets_used.name: PoxData Dataset
datasets_used.description: A supplementary dataset incorporated to expand viral skin lesion coverage. Only cowpox images were extracted from this source.

use.training: Source for curated final dataset; only cowpox images were used.

datasets_used.name: Multi-Class Viral Skin Lesion Dataset (MCVSLD)
datasets_used.description: A dataset from Mendeley Data providing extensive viral skin lesion imagery. Only cowpox images were extracted from this source to ensure consistency in viral class representation.

use.training: Source for curated final dataset; only cowpox images were used.

datasets_used.name: Final Curated Dataset
datasets_used.description: The final experimental dataset comprising 2,039 dermoscopic and clinical images, manually annotated and labeled using Roboflow. It was partitioned into training, validation, and testing subsets following an 87%-8%-4% split.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv12-seg model.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and validation.
use.testing: Used for unbiased performance assessment and evaluation of mAP, precision, and recall.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a detailed view of class-wise prediction performance and illustrate the proportion of correctly classified instances for each skin lesion category.
To illustrate the model’s capacity to balance precision and recall across varying confidence thresholds and determine the optimal confidence threshold for segmentation masks.
To evaluate detection and segmentation performance using mean Average Precision at an IoU threshold of 0.5 and across multiple IoU thresholds, respectively.
To assess the balance between sensitivity and specificity, indicating a trade-off in detection and segmentation accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using deterministic metrics such as mAP@50, mAP@50–95, precision, recall, and normalized confusion matrices.

Final Curated Dataset (Training Split).notes: Training was conducted over 66 epochs. The model achieved high inference efficiency requiring 1.8 ms per image for forward prediction and 1.9 ms for post-processing.

Final Curated Dataset (Validation Split).notes: The text describes validation results indicating strong and balanced detection and segmentation performance. Note: The JSON structure in the source text for Table II (Validation Segmentation) appears to have overlapping values with Table I (Training Bounding Box) or contains typos in the provided context chunks (e.g., Chunk 11 and 12 show identical values for 'all' and class-wise metrics in both tables). The values above reflect the explicit numbers listed under 'TABLE II. SEGMENTATION RESULTS IN TRAINING' in Chunk 12, which seem to be mislabeled or duplicated in the source text relative to standard reporting. However, Chunk 11 explicitly states 'Validation results (Table 2)... achieved a mean Average Precision of 0.872 at an IoU threshold of 0.5 (mAP@50) and 0.589 across multiple IoU thresholds (mAP@50–95), accompanied by a precision of 0.866 and a recall of 0.821.' The class-wise values in Chunk 12 under 'TABLE II' match the 'TABLE I' values in Chunk 11. Given the ambiguity, the overall metrics are prioritized as they are explicitly linked to validation in the text.

Final Curated Dataset (Testing Split).notes: Class-wise analysis showed that Melanocytic Nevi and Vascular Lesions attained the strongest performance. Cowpox and Dermatofibroma exhibited comparatively lower detection and segmentation accuracy, likely due to residual class imbalance and subtle inter-class visual similarities. Qualitative results confirmed accurate lesion localization and well-defined segmentation boundaries.

preprocessing_techniques: Manual annotation and labeling using Roboflow platform with visual verification by human annotators
Resizing to uniform resolution of 832 × 832 pixels
Data augmentation: random cropping with up to 30% zoom
Data augmentation: horizontal and vertical shearing up to ±10°
Data augmentation: grayscale conversion applied to 23% of samples
Data augmentation: photometric adjustments including hue (±25°), saturation (−32% to +32%), and exposure (−15% to +15%)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a geometric transform during preprocessing/augmentation.
Horizontal and vertical shearing applied up to ±10°.
Hue adjusted by ±25°, saturation by −32% to +32%, and exposure by −15% to +15%.

conclusions: The proposed deep transfer learning-based framework using the YOLOv12-seg architecture is effective for automated multi-class skin lesion recognition and instance segmentation.
The model achieved an overall mAP@50 of 0.816 and an mAP@50–95 of 0.514 on the held-out test set, with precision and recall values of 0.778 and 0.750, respectively.

Class-wise analysis demonstrated that Vascular Lesions and Melanocytic Nevi attained the strongest detection and segmentation performance, while Cowpox and Dermatofibroma exhibited comparatively lower accuracy.

The lower performance for Cowpox and Dermatofibroma is attributed to residual class imbalance and subtle inter-class visual similarities, such as overlapping morphological characteristics and chromatic patterns.

The model exhibited high computational efficiency, requiring an average of 1.8 ms for forward prediction and 1.9 ms for post-processing per image, making it suitable for real-time clinical applications.

Qualitative results confirmed accurate lesion localization and well-defined segmentation boundaries across diverse imaging conditions, including variations in skin tone, illumination, and lesion scale.

The system was successfully deployed via a Streamlit-based web application, demonstrating its practicality and potential for integration into real-time clinical decision support workflows for dermatological diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, curated dataset of only 2,039 images, which limits the generalizability of the deep learning model compared to large-scale benchmarks.
The train-validation-test split is heavily skewed (87%-8%-4%), resulting in a very small test set (102 images) for unbiased performance assessment.

The model was evaluated exclusively on four specific classes (Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions, Cowpox, Dermatofibroma), excluding critical malignant classes like Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma, which restricts its clinical utility for cancer screening.
Performance disparities were observed between classes, with Cowpox and Dermatofibroma showing lower accuracy due to residual class imbalance and subtle inter-class visual similarities.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on deterministic metrics like mAP and precision/recall.
External validation on independent, multi-center datasets was not performed; the model was only tested on data derived from the same curated sources (ISIC, SKIN-DS, etc.).

While deployed via a Streamlit web application, the system is presented as a proof-of-concept for clinical decision support rather than a validated clinical tool, lacking prospective clinical trial evidence.
